lesson: Attach a concrete suggested fix to every review comment instead of just flagging the problem, so the author can act without a follow-up round-trip.
Scope a review to the diff itself; if it bundles an unrelated refactor, ask the author to split it into a separate commit or PR first.
Flag mutable default arguments like def f(x=[]) — the default object is created once at definition time and shared across every call, leaking state silently.
Trace every return and exception path from a resource acquisition to its release; leaks hide in early returns or caught-exception branches that skip cleanup.
Check loop and slice boundaries against a concrete tiny case (n=0, n=1, empty input) instead of trusting intuition — off-by-ones hide in < vs <= and inclusive ranges.
Flag a clever one-liner that needs a second read to parse even when it is correct — the readability tax compounds across every future reader.
Review a test by mentally reverting the fix it covers; if the test would still pass without the code change, it isn't actually testing the bug.
Prefix non-blocking style comments with 'nit:' to separate must-fix issues from preference, so trivial feedback never stalls a mergeable change.
Check that test assertions target observable behavior, not internal call sequences — over-mocked tests break on valid refactors that leave output unchanged.
